CCR T14 §5.85(a)(1) — Trout, General Statewide Regulations (inland lakes, reservoirs, and ponds)
sources.quotedText: All inland lakes, reservoirs, and ponds, including those on private lands, are open to fishing all year with a five-trout daily bag limit and ten-trout possession limit.

2026 California Freshwater Sport Fishing Regulations (Updated 06/01/2026)
sources.quotedText: All inland lakes, reservoirs, and ponds, including those on private lands, are open to fishing all year with a five-trout daily bag limit and 10 trout possession limit.

sources.title: California Fish and Game Code §7145 — Sport Fishing License Requirement
sources.quotedText: Except as otherwise provided in this article, every person 16 years of age or older who takes any fish, reptile, or amphibian for any purpose other than profit shall first obtain a valid license for that purpose...

sources.title: CDFW Memorandum — Pre-Stocking Evaluation at Lola Montez Lakes, Nevada County (Nov. 9, 2020)
sources.quotedText: The California Department of Fish and Wildlife (CDFW) has been stocking Upper Lola Montez Lake with Rainbow Trout (Oncorhynchus mykiss; RT) since at least 1967 and most recently stocked Lower Lola Montez Lake with RT in 2018. ... Since 2012, CDFW has planted sterile, triploid RT in Lower Lola Montez Lake. ... Results of the VES and gill net set suggest that Lower Lola Montez Lake is an ideal location for an aerially stocked put-and-grow RT fishery.

sources.quotedText: This trail passes through private property. Please be respectful and stay on trail. No camping or fires on private property.

parameters.note: Lola Montez Lake (Lower) is not listed in CCR §7.40(b) or §7.50(b), so it falls under the general statewide lake regulation in §5.85(a)(1).

parameters.description: Lower Lola Montez Lake (Lake ID 13212) is a backcountry put-and-grow Rainbow Trout fishery on Tahoe National Forest land, reached via the ~3-mile Lower Lola Montez Trail from the I-80 Soda Springs exit. CDFW has stocked it with Rainbow Trout historically (most recently 2018) and, since 2012, has used sterile triploid Rainbow Trout to prevent reproduction; a July 2020 CDFW gill-net and visual-encounter survey confirmed surviving Rainbow Trout (up to ~15 inches) and incidental Golden Shiner present, with no evidence of trout reproduction. This distinct lower lake is separate from Upper Lola Montez Lake (Lake ID 13202), which is difficult to access, was last stocked with Brook Trout in 1999, and may now be fishless. Lola Montez Lake (Lower) is not named in CDFW's current 2026 Provisional Stocking Document, so no scheduled 2026 plants are confirmed as of this writing.

Since 2012, CDFW has planted sterile, triploid RT in Lower Lola Montez Lake.
